name: Alternating Current
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. AC Source and RMS Values
sections.body:
An alternating EMF varies sinusoidally with time: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$, where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency, $\varepsilon_0$ the peak EMF. In India $f = 50$ Hz.

Average over a full cycle: $\langle I\rangle = 0$. Useful average is the root-mean-square value: $$I_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,I_0$$ Same: $V_{\text{rms}} = V_0/\sqrt{2}$.

RMS values are what AC voltmeters and ammeters read; AC power formulae use RMS. India's 220 V supply means $V_{\text{rms}} = 220$ V, peak $V_0 = 311$ V.

sections.heading: 2. AC Through Pure Resistor
sections.body:
Source $V = V_0\sin\omega t$ across resistor $R$: by Ohm's law $I = V_0/R \sin\omega t = I_0\sin\omega t$. Current and voltage are in phase.

Average power: $P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} = I_{\text{rms}}^2 R = V_{\text{rms}}^2/R$. Resistor dissipates power exactly as in DC, with RMS values.

sections.heading: 3. AC Through Pure Inductor
sections.body:
For source $V = V_0\sin\omega t$ across inductor $L$: $V = L\,dI/dt$, so $$I = \dfrac{V_0}{\omega L}\sin\!\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ Current lags voltage by $\pi/2$. Peak current $I_0 = V_0/(\omega L)$.

The quantity $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ is the inductive reactance (unit Ω). It increases with frequency. At DC ($f = 0$), $X_L = 0$ — pure inductor is a short.

Average power: $\langle P\rangle = 0$ — a pure inductor consumes no real power, only stores and returns energy.

sections.heading: 4. AC Through Pure Capacitor
sections.body:
For source $V = V_0\sin\omega t$ across capacitor $C$: $q = CV$, $I = dq/dt = CV_0\omega\cos\omega t$, so $$I = \dfrac{V_0}{1/(\omega C)}\sin\!\left(\omega t + \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ Current leads voltage by $\pi/2$.

Capacitive reactance: $X_C = 1/(\omega C) = 1/(2\pi f C)$. Decreases with frequency. At DC, $X_C \to \infty$ — capacitor blocks DC.

Average power: zero. Capacitor only stores and returns energy.

sections.heading: 5. Series LCR Circuit and Impedance
sections.body:
For $R$, $L$, $C$ in series with source $V = V_0\sin\omega t$: voltages across each element are out of phase. Use phasor diagram: $V_R$ in phase with $I$, $V_L$ leads by 90°, $V_C$ lags by 90°.

Net voltage: $V_0 = \sqrt{V_R^2 + (V_L - V_C)^2}$. Impedance: $$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$$ Peak current: $I_0 = V_0/Z$. Phase angle (current relative to voltage): $$\tan\phi = \dfrac{X_L - X_C}{R}$$ If $X_L > X_C$: circuit inductive, current lags. If $X_C > X_L$: capacitive, current leads.

sections.heading: 6. Resonance in Series LCR
sections.body:
At resonance, $X_L = X_C$, i.e. $\omega L = 1/(\omega C)$, giving the resonant angular frequency $$\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}, \quad f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

At resonance: $Z = R$ (minimum), $I_0 = V_0/R$ (maximum), phase $\phi = 0$ (in phase). Sharp peak in current vs frequency.

Quality factor $Q = \omega_0 L/R = 1/(\omega_0 RC)$. High $Q$ → sharper resonance, narrower bandwidth $\Delta\omega = R/L$. Used in tuning radios.

Note: at resonance, $V_L$ and $V_C$ can each be much larger than the source voltage — they cancel.

sections.heading: 7. Power in AC Circuits — Power Factor
sections.body:
Average power in an AC circuit: $$P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\phi$$ where $\cos\phi$ is the power factor, with $\cos\phi = R/Z$ for series LCR.

Cases:

  • Pure resistor: $\phi = 0$, $\cos\phi = 1$, $P = V_{\text{rms}}I_{\text{rms}}$ (full power).
  • Pure $L$ or $C$: $\phi = \pm\pi/2$, $\cos\phi = 0$, $P = 0$ (wattless current).
  • Series LCR at resonance: $\phi = 0$, full power.

Low power factor wastes capacity; industries use power-factor correction (typically banks of capacitors) to bring $\cos\phi$ near 1.

sections.heading: 8. Transformer
sections.body:
A device based on mutual induction that changes AC voltage. Primary coil ($N_p$ turns) and secondary coil ($N_s$ turns) are wound on a common laminated soft-iron core.

For an ideal transformer (no losses): $$\dfrac{V_s}{V_p} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}, \quad \dfrac{I_s}{I_p} = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s}$$ so $V_p I_p = V_s I_s$ — power is conserved.

Step-up: $N_s > N_p$ → $V_s > V_p$, $I_s < I_p$. Step-down: opposite.

Energy losses (real transformers, ~99% efficient): copper loss ($I^2 R$ in windings), iron loss (hysteresis and eddy currents in core), flux leakage. Mitigated by thick low-resistance windings, laminated soft-iron core (low coercivity, low resistivity).

Used in long-distance transmission: step up at generator (low current → low $I^2 R$ loss in transmission lines), step down for distribution.

summary: AC quantities described by RMS: $V_{\text{rms}} = V_0/\sqrt{2}$. Resistor: in phase. Inductor: $X_L = \omega L$, current lags by 90°. Capacitor: $X_C = 1/\omega C$, current leads by 90°. Series LCR: $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L-X_C)^2}$, resonance at $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$. Power $P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}\cos\phi$. Transformer: $V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p$.
pitfalls: Average current over a full cycle is ZERO; use RMS for power.
Inductor: current LAGS voltage by π/2; capacitor: current LEADS by π/2 (mnemonic: ICE for capacitor, ELI for inductor — E before I in inductor).
Pure L or C consumes ZERO real power, but carries 'wattless' current.
At resonance, V_L and V_C individually can exceed source voltage but cancel each other.
Transformer works only on AC — DC produces no flux change, no induced EMF.
Step-up transformer increases voltage but DECREASES current proportionally; power is conserved (in ideal case).
